name: Nintendo Entertainment System
description: The NES was Nintendo's breakthrough home console, launching classic franchises like Mario and Zelda. Its pixel graphics and chiptune sound defined a generation of gaming worldwide.
funFact: The Famicom, the Japanese version, had a microphone in the controller.

codingExamples.title: Change Background Color (6502 Assembly)
explanation: LDA #$01 loads immediate value 1 into the accumulator.
STA $2001 stores the value in the PPU Mask register to change display settings (like background color).

websites.title: NESDev Wiki
websites.description: Comprehensive technical documentation for NES development.

websites.title: Making NES Games
websites.description: Simple online 6502 assembler and tutorials.

name: Super Nintendo Entertainment System
description: The SNES delivered 16-bit graphics, Mode 7 effects and classic games like Super Mario World and Chrono Trigger. It helped shape the golden age of console gaming.
funFact: Mode 7 graphics powered the 3D-effect backgrounds in games like Mario Kart.

codingExamples.title: Set CGRAM Color (65816 Assembly)
codingExamples.code:
LDA #$7FFF ; white
STA $2122 ; write to CGRAM
explanation: LDA #$7FFF loads the color white in SNES format.
STA $2122 writes the color value to the CGRAM register to set background/text colors.

websites.title: SNES Development Wiki
websites.description: Collaborative wiki about SNES programming and hardware.

name: Nintendo 64
description: The N64 brought 3D graphics and iconic titles like Super Mario 64 and The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time. Its trident controller was a memorable part of its legacy.
funFact: Nintendo 64 carts could hold up to 64MB of data, huge for the time.

codingExamples.title: Print to Screen (C with Libdragon)
explanation: Includes N64-specific libraries for development (e.g., Libdragon for homebrew).
Initializes the N64 console and prints a message to the screen.

websites.title: Libdragon - N64 Homebrew SDK
websites.description: Popular open-source library for making homebrew N64 games.

name: PlayStation 1
description: The original PlayStation revolutionized gaming with 3D graphics and memorable franchises like Final Fantasy and Gran Turismo. Its vast library cemented its legacy as a 90s icon.
funFact: The PlayStation 1 controller didn't have analog sticks until the DualShock revision.

codingExamples.title: Hello World (C)
explanation: Includes the PSX hardware libraries.
Prints a message to the PlayStation debug console or screen.

websites.title: PSXDEV.net
websites.description: Community and guides for PlayStation 1 development and homebrew.

name: PlayStation 2
description: The PS2 is the best-selling console of all time, supporting an immense library of games. Its backwards compatibility and DVD support made it a household staple.
funFact: PS2s were once networked together to form a supercomputer by researchers at the US Air Force.

codingExamples.title: Draw Rectangle (C, PS2SDK)
explanation: gsKit is a PS2 homebrew graphics library.
Initializes screen and clears it with a solid color (green, using ARGB hex).

websites.title: PS2Dev
websites.description: Home for PlayStation 2 development resources and forums.

name: Xbox (Original)
description: Microsoft's first foray into consoles, the Xbox, boasted a built-in hard drive and iconic titles like Halo. Its robust hardware set it apart from competitors.
funFact: Duke Nukem Forever was in development throughout the entire original Xbox lifespan.

codingExamples.title: Hello World (C++)
explanation: Uses Xbox debug libraries.
Repeatedly prints a Hello message to the debug output.

websites.title: XboxDevWiki
websites.description: Extensive documentation of the original Xbox hardware and OS.

name: Nintendo GameCube
description: The GameCube was Nintendo's entry into the 21st-century console wars, featuring compact discs, cube-shaped design, and favorites like Super Smash Bros. Melee.
funFact: The GameCube could connect to Game Boy Advance games for bonus features in titles like Zelda: Four Swords.

codingExamples.title: Change Screen Color (C with libogc)
explanation: Uses libogc for GameCube homebrew.
Initializes video and framebuffer, switches display to active, and enters infinite loop.

websites.title: DevKitPro GameCube Dev
websites.description: Setup and starting guides for GameCube homebrew development.

name: Sega Genesis
description: The Sega Genesis was famous for its blast processing and Sonic the Hedgehog series. It was the rival to Nintendo's SNES during the 16-bit era.
funFact: Michael Jackson reportedly contributed music to Sonic 3.

codingExamples.title: Set Background Palette (68K Assembly)
explanation: Writes the color blue to the background palette register.
This simple line sets the background color for the entire screen.

websites.title: Genesis Development Wiki
websites.description: Collated hardware information and dev tools for Genesis programming.

name: Game Boy
description: Game Boy made gaming portable, with millions of users enjoying Tetris and Pokémon on the go. Its simple monochrome display helped games run for hours on AA batteries.
funFact: Game Boy's greenish screen was chosen for low power consumption.

codingExamples.title: Infinite Loop (Z80 Assembly)
explanation: Defines a label and jumps to it, creating an infinite loop. Useful as a 'do nothing' placeholder.

websites.title: GBDev Wiki
websites.description: Modern and friendly docs for Game Boy homebrew creation.

name: Game Boy Advance
description: The Game Boy Advance brought 32-bit power to handhelds, enabling vivid color graphics and large worlds in your pocket. Popular franchises like Pokémon and Zelda thrived here.
funFact: The GBA can play all original Game Boy and Game Boy Color games.

codingExamples.title: Set Mode 3 Pixel (C)
explanation: Sets GBA display mode to Mode 3 (bitmap, 240x160).
Directly draws a white pixel at screen center.

websites.title: TONC GBA Programming Guide
websites.description: The classic reference text for GBA software development.

name: Nintendo DS
description: The DS popularized dual screens and touchscreen gaming with blockbuster franchises and quirky innovations. Its Wi-Fi connectivity introduced global gaming on the go.
funFact: The Nintendo DS Lite was originally codenamed 'Nitro.'

codingExamples.title: Print Message on Screen (C, libnds)
explanation: Initializes the text console and prints a quick message to the DS top screen.

websites.title: DevKitPro NDS Dev
websites.description: Official docs for homebrew programming on the Nintendo DS.

name: PlayStation Portable
description: Sony's PSP brought powerful 3D graphics and media playback to portable gaming. It fostered an active scene of both official and homebrew games.
funFact: Homebrew developers famously cracked the PSP's security, enabling custom apps and emulators.

codingExamples.title: Print Message (C, PSP SDK)
explanation: Initializes debugging screen and prints message.
Sleeps the main thread, keeping the output visible.

websites.title: PSP Programming Wiki
websites.description: Community-maintained SDK docs and homebrew programming advice.

name: Game Gear
description: Sega's color handheld, the Game Gear, offered fast-paced portable titles and competed directly against the Game Boy. Its bright display set it apart.
funFact: Accessories included a TV tuner for watching broadcast television on the go.

codingExamples.title: Main Loop (Z80 Assembly)
explanation: A main loop is a common way to keep programs running on Game Gear.
This code loops forever.

websites.title: SMS Power!
websites.description: Guides for Sega Game Gear (and Master System) homebrew software.

name: Cisco IOS Router
description: Cisco IOS routers form the backbone of the internet, enabling enterprise networking and secure data transport. They use a command-line interface for configuration.
funFact: The 'write memory' command is still supported, despite being a legacy command.

codingExamples.title: Set Hostname
explanation: Enters global configuration mode.
Sets the hostname to Router01.
Exits configuration mode.

websites.title: Cisco CLI Analyzer
websites.description: Interactive online tool for learning Cisco CLI commands.

name: Linksys WRT54G
description: The WRT54G was a popular SOHO router, famous for its hackable Linux-based firmware. It enabled advanced customizations for home networking.
funFact: The openness of the original firmware inspired projects like DD-WRT and OpenWRT.

codingExamples.title: Check Router Uptime (Telnet Shell)
explanation: Outputs how long the router has been running since last boot.

websites.title: DD-WRT Homepage
websites.description: Popular alternate firmware supported on many Linksys routers.

name: DD-WRT Router
description: DD-WRT is open firmware for routers, providing advanced features like QoS, VLANs, and VPN. It is a favorite among tinkerers and prosumers.
funFact: DD-WRT can run on over 100 router models, not just Linksys.

codingExamples.title: Enable SSH Access
codingExamples.code:
nvram set sshd_enable=1
nvram commit
reboot
explanation: Enables SSH on the router for secure command-line access.
Commits the setting and reboots.

websites.title: DD-WRT Wiki
websites.description: Tech guides and documentation for DD-WRT routers.

name: Raspberry Pi as Router
description: A Raspberry Pi can be configured as a network router or access point, teaching networking skills hands-on and enabling custom firewall, VPN, and monitoring tools.
funFact: The Pi can serve as the complete home network hub, firewall, and even traffic visualizer.

codingExamples.title: Print Local IP Address
codingExamples.code:
import socket
hostname = socket.gethostname()
local_ip = socket.gethostbyname(hostname)
print(f"Local IP: {local_ip}")
explanation: Imports the socket library.
Gets the system's hostname and uses it to look up the local IP address.
Prints the result. This can be run on the Pi to confirm network status.

websites.title: Pi My Life Up - Router Guide
websites.description: Step-by-step tutorial: turning your Pi into a Wi-Fi router/access point.

name: Commodore 64
description: The C64 was the world's most popular home computer in the 1980s. It introduced millions to programming, games, and music creation, earning a huge community even today.
funFact: Its SID sound chip inspired an entire genre of electronic music known as 'chiptune.'

codingExamples.title: Hello World (BASIC)
explanation: Line 10 prints 'HELLO, WORLD!' to the screen.
Line 20 causes the program to repeat, creating an infinite scroll.

websites.title: C64 Wiki BASIC Programming
websites.description: Tutorials and programming examples for Commodore 64 BASIC.

name: Apple II
description: The Apple II was a pioneering personal computer, famed for ease of use and expandability. It helped spark the school computing revolution in the US.
funFact: The first version of VisiCalc, the spreadsheet app, debuted on the Apple II.

codingExamples.title: Input and Output (BASIC)
explanation: Line 10 prompts the user to input a name and stores it.
Line 20 prints a friendly greeting using the name entered.

websites.title: Apple2.org BASIC
websites.description: BASIC reference, tutorials, and emulator links for Apple II users.

name: Raspberry Pi
description: The Raspberry Pi is a small, cheap, and popular microcomputer that makes coding and electronics accessible to everyone. With a lively community and endless tutorials, it's perfect for beginners.
funFact: Over 40 million Raspberry Pis have been sold globally.

codingExamples.title: Blink an LED (Python, GPIO Zero)
codingExamples.code:
from gpiozero import LED
from time import sleep
led = LED(17)
while True:
    led.on()
    sleep(1)
    led.off()
    sleep(1)
explanation: Imports the gpiozero library for simple GPIO pin control.
Sets up GPIO pin 17 for output to an LED.
Turns the LED on for 1 second, off for 1 second, and repeats.
This is a common beginner hardware project with Raspberry Pi.

codingExamples.title: Simple Hello World (Python)
explanation: This one-liner prints a friendly hello message to the screen.
It's the classic starting point for all coding beginners on the Pi.

websites.title: Official Raspberry Pi Learning
websites.description: Beginner-friendly projects, code samples, and explanations from the official Raspberry Pi Foundation.

name: ZX Spectrum
description: The ZX Spectrum was a defining British home microcomputer whose rubber keys and colorful graphics became iconic. It was the birthplace of many classic 8-bit games and coders.
funFact: The original ZX Spectrum beep sound was used in British TV for years after.

codingExamples.title: Draw Diagonal Line (BASIC)
codingExamples.code:
10 FOR I=0 TO 20
20 PLOT I,I
30 NEXT I
explanation: Line 10 starts a for loop from 0 to 20.
Line 20 draws a pixel at position (I,I) creating a diagonal line.
Line 30 increments to the next step.

websites.title: ZX Spectrum Programming
websites.description: Official Sinclair BASIC manual for ZX Spectrum.

name: MAME Arcade Cabinet
description: MAME (Multiple Arcade Machine Emulator) cabinets let you run, play, and even develop games for hundreds of classic arcade systems, all in one retro-style shell.
funFact: MAME is open-source, preserving arcade games through emulation.

codingExamples.title: List Available Games
explanation: Executes the mame binary with the -listfull argument.
Outputs a full list of available arcade games detected by your ROM set.

websites.title: Official MAME Dev
websites.description: Home of official MAME downloads, docs, and news.

name: Neo Geo MVS
description: The Neo Geo brought arcade-quality graphics to cabinets and homes alike. Known for fighting games like King of Fighters and Metal Slug.
funFact: Neo Geo cartridges could cost as much as $300 at launch.

codingExamples.title: Set Palette Color (68K Assembly)
explanation: Moves a hex color value (red) into a palette memory address.
Changes a palette color for sprites/backgrounds.

websites.title: NeoDev Wiki
websites.description: Development resources and guides for Neo Geo programming.

name: Pac-Man Hardware
description: Pac-Man ran on custom arcade hardware, famed for its simple graphics and addictive gameplay. Modders use the platform to create their own mazes and versions.
funFact: Pac-Man's original title, 'Puck Man,' was changed for the US release.

codingExamples.title: Infinite Loop (Z80 Assembly)
explanation: A simple infinite loop, typical for halting the CPU after main game loop ends.

websites.title: Pac-Man Dossier
websites.description: A deep technical analysis of Pac-Man's algorithms, hardware and game logic.
